brief_description: The Eternal City where ancient ruins stand alongside Renaissance masterpieces and vibrant modern life unfolds in cobblestoned piazzas. Rome is a living museum spanning nearly 3,000 years—from the Colosseum to the Vatican, from neighborhood trattorias to baroque fountains, every corner reveals another layer of history, art, and la dolce vita.

sections.title: Why Rome
sections.content: Few cities carry the weight of history like Rome. This is where Western civilization took shape—where emperors built monuments to their glory, where the Catholic Church established its seat, where Renaissance masters created works that still draw millions. But Rome isn't a museum; it's a living city where locals drink espresso at marble bars, Vespas weave through ancient streets, and dinner doesn't start until 9pm.

sections.title: The Neighborhoods
sections.content: Centro Storico holds the postcard highlights—Piazza Navona, the Pantheon, Trevi Fountain—amid tourist crowds and overpriced cafés. Trastevere across the Tiber feels like a village, with ivy-covered walls and trattorias that fill with Romans after dark. Testaccio, the old slaughterhouse district, remains authentically local with the city's best traditional cooking. Monti, once working-class, now draws a younger crowd to its wine bars and vintage shops. The Jewish Ghetto, one of Europe's oldest, offers fried artichokes and quiet piazzas.

sections.title: Getting Around
sections.content: Rome's Metro is limited—just three lines that miss many major sights—but the historic center is compact enough to walk. Budget an hour to cross from the Spanish Steps to Trastevere on foot, stopping for espresso and gelato along the way. Buses connect the rest but can be confusing and crowded; the 64 and 40 to the Vatican are notorious for pickpockets. Taxis are reasonable within the center; use official white cars with meters.

why_visit: Ancient Rome brought to life—the Colosseum, Roman Forum, and Palatine Hill tell 2,000 years of history
The Vatican Museums and Sistine Chapel house priceless Renaissance masterpieces
Authentic Italian cuisine at its source—carbonara, cacio e pepe, and supplì done right
Baroque fountains and piazzas perfect for evening strolls and people-watching
Living history where every neighborhood reveals different eras of Western civilization

practical_info.tipping: Not expected; round up for good service
practical_info.tap_water: Excellent and safe—use the nasoni public fountains

seasonal_notes.spring: Perfect weather (15-22°C), Easter crowds, blooming gardens. Book Vatican ahead.
seasonal_notes.summer: Hot (28-35°C) and crowded. Romans flee in August; some restaurants close.
seasonal_notes.fall: Ideal conditions (15-25°C), harvest season, film festivals, manageable crowds.
seasonal_notes.winter: Mild (5-12°C), short queues, Christmas markets, occasional rain.

best_time_to_visit.overall: April-June and September-October offer the best balance of weather, crowds, and atmosphere
best_time_to_visit.budget: November-February for lowest prices and shortest queues
best_time_to_visit.weather: May and September for warm days without summer's oppressive heat
best_time_to_visit.events: Holy Week for Easter ceremonies, late August for Ferragosto exodus

Best in Summer.activity: Evening Aperitivo at a Rooftop Bar

Local Experiences.activity: Sunday at Porta Portese Flea Market
